narrative_blocks.header: Mother of an author and a Union general
narrative_blocks.body: Catherine Wadsworth married Nathaniel Terry, one of Hartford's most prominent citizens, in 1798. The Hooker-Talcott blood that ran through her passed to her grandchildren: Rose Terry Cooke became one of New England's most celebrated regional authors, and Gen. Alfred Howe Terry led the Union assault on Fort Wagner and later commanded the Department of the Dakotas — present at the aftermath of the Little Bighorn. Catherine never lived to see either of them distinguish themselves; she died in 1841.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of an author and a Union general
narrative_blocks.body: Catherine Wadsworth married Nathaniel Terry, one of Hartford's most prominent citizens, in 1798. The Hooker-Talcott blood that ran through her passed to her grandchildren: Rose Terry Cooke became one of New England's most celebrated regional authors, and Gen. Alfred Howe Terry led the Union assault on Fort Wagner and later commanded the Department of the Dakotas — present at the aftermath of the Little Bighorn. Catherine never lived to see either of them distinguish themselves; she died in 1841.

notable.notable_blurb: 4th Mayor Hartford; US Congressman; bank president

burial.plot_notes: d. New Haven CT. Burial location unverified — possibly Grove Street Cemetery New Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Mayor of Hartford for seven turbulent years
narrative_blocks.body: He served as the 4th Mayor of Hartford from 1824 to 1831, spanning the city's transition from Federalist to Whig politics. He had previously served one term in the US House of Representatives (1817-1819) as a Federalist, and was a delegate to the 1818 Connecticut Constitutional Convention.

narrative_blocks.header: President of Hartford Fire Insurance for 25 years
narrative_blocks.body: He served as president of the Hartford Fire Insurance Company from 1810 to 1835 — a 25-year tenure spanning the company's early growth into one of New England's leading insurers. He simultaneously served as president of the Hartford Bank from 1819 to 1828.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale-educated landscape gardener of Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale College, Henry returned to Hartford and worked as a landscape gardener — a refined avocation more than a commercial trade in the 1820s. He is buried at Collinsville Cemetery, named for the village where his daughter Alice married Howard Collins.

notable.notable_blurb: 19th-century New England regional realist; Atlantic Monthly mainstay

cross_connections.display_label: Stowe defended her authorship against pen-name imposters

narrative_blocks.header: Appeared in the first Atlantic Monthly
narrative_blocks.body: Her short story 'Sally Parsons's Duty' ran in the inaugural November 1857 issue of The Atlantic Monthly, before the magazine had built a reputation. She published over two hundred stories and poems across thirty years in the Atlantic, Harper's, Putnam's, and Scribner's.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a man sixteen years her junior
narrative_blocks.body: At forty-six she married Rollin Hillyer Cooke, then thirty, after both her parents had died. Her writing income supported the household — Rollin's banking ventures repeatedly failed.

narrative_blocks.header: Father's mulberry-tree speculation bankrupted the family
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Henry Wadsworth Terry lost the family property in the 1839 Morus Multicaulis crash, the speculative mulberry-tree bubble that wiped out hundreds of Connecticut investors hoping to launch a domestic silk industry. The family moved to Hartford and into the brick mansion her great-grandfather Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth had built in 1799 for her grandmother.

narrative_blocks.header: Pioneered New England's regional realist tradition
narrative_blocks.body: She wrote about Connecticut farm women in plain dialect and unsentimental detail decades before such writing became fashionable. Critics later credited her as the precursor Sarah Orne Jewett and Mary Wilkins Freeman drew from.

narrative_blocks.header: Greek refugee servant filled her childhood with ghosts
narrative_blocks.body: The family servant Athanasius had escaped a Turkish massacre in Greece and filled her young nights with ghost stories. Her mother Anne Hurlbut, raised under what Cooke later called 'the severest puritanical habits,' kept her daughter's reading strict but could not silence the imagination such tales planted.

education.notes: Graduated 1843 at age sixteen. Studied literature and composition with principal John P. Brace. Catharine Beecher had founded the school in 1823.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale College 1820; Yale Medical degree 1823
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated from Yale College in 1820 — the second son of General Nathaniel Terry (Yale 1786) and Catherine Wadsworth. He continued at the Yale Medical School and received his MD in spring 1823.

narrative_blocks.header: Died suddenly at 43 in his native city
narrative_blocks.body: He practiced medicine in Hartford for two decades after settling there following Yale. He died very suddenly on December 22, 1843, in his 44th year. His widow Sophia survived him by 25 years.

notable.notable_blurb: US Navy Commander; Civil War; Naval Academy Commandant

narrative_blocks.header: Fought at Mobile Bay, New Orleans, and Vicksburg
narrative_blocks.body: As a junior officer aboard the steam sloop USS Richmond, he served throughout the Civil War with the Western Gulf Blockading Squadron. He fought in the engagement with the CSS Manassas, the capture of New Orleans, the gauntlet at Vicksburg, the bombardment at Port Hudson, and the Battle of Mobile Bay.

narrative_blocks.header: Naval Academy Commandant of Midshipmen 1875-1878
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Commandant of Midshipmen at the United States Naval Academy from 1875 to 1878 — the chief discipline and training officer for the entire midshipman corps. He had previously commanded the screw steamship USS Saco with the Asiatic Fleet from 1870 to 1872.

narrative_blocks.header: Two USS Terry destroyers were named for him
narrative_blocks.body: Two United States Navy ships have been named USS Terry in his honor — the destroyer DD-25 (commissioned 1910, served WWI) and a later destroyer DD-513. He died of tuberculosis at Manitou Springs in 1882 and is buried at the U.S. Naval Academy Cemetery.

cross_connections.display_label: First cousin; both Civil War officers leading the charge
Both fought at Mobile Bay under Admiral Farragut, 1864

military_service.unit: USS Richmond, Western Gulf Blockading Squadron
battles: Battle of the Head of Passes (1861)
military_service.notes: Career US Navy officer 1853-1881. Naval Academy Commandant of Midshipmen 1875-1878.

burial.plot_notes: United States Naval Academy Cemetery, Annapolis MD. Granite stone column over seven feet tall, made to look as if broken off at jagged angle. Inscription: 'Commander Edward Terry who fought valiantly at the battles of Mobile Bay, Vicksburg, and New Orleans.'

sources.label: CT Hale Collection of Cemetery Inscriptions and Newspaper Notices, 1629-1934

narrative_blocks.header: Civil War veteran, Co. E. 17th US Infantry
narrative_blocks.body: Clarence Terry served in Company E of the 17th United States Infantry during the Civil War. He died at 45 in Columbus, Georgia, and was buried at Spring Grove Cemetery, Hartford — separate from his parents at Old North.

burial.plot_notes: Spring Grove Cemetery, Hartford CT. Inscription: 'Veteran of the Civil War (Co. E. 17th U.S. I.) Aged 45 years.'

institutions.institution_blurb: Studied law before turning to military career as Union general.

sources.label: Hurlbut genealogy book / online vital records

cross_connections.display_label: First cousin; Navy commander at Mobile Bay and New Orleans

narrative_blocks.header: Captured Fort Fisher with two thousand prisoners
narrative_blocks.body: In January 1865 he commanded the second expedition against Fort Fisher after Butler's failure, capturing the Confederate stronghold along with over 2,000 prisoners and 167 cannon. He was promoted to Brigadier General in the regular army for the victory.

narrative_blocks.header: Self-taught general with no military training
narrative_blocks.body: He was a New Haven court clerk and Yale Law graduate when the Civil War began, with no prior military experience. He has been called one of the most capable generals to emerge from the war without previous training.

narrative_blocks.header: Sent Custer up the Rosebud at Little Bighorn
narrative_blocks.body: As overall commander of the 1876 Centennial Campaign, he wrote orders sending Custer up the Rosebud on June 22. His column arrived after the massacre, sparking decades of dispute over whether Custer had disobeyed his orders.

narrative_blocks.header: Last military governor of Atlanta opposed the Klan
narrative_blocks.body: In 1868 he negotiated the Treaty of Fort Laramie that ended Red Cloud's War. He then served as the last military governor of the Third Military District at Atlanta in 1869, where he was a strong opponent of the Ku Klux Klan.

narrative_blocks.header: His line ended at New Haven, no children
narrative_blocks.body: He died at New Haven in December 1890 at age 63, having never had children. His siblings included Harriet Wadsworth Terry and Jeremiah Wadsworth Terry, names their father chose for the Wadsworth-Hooker ancestry.

education.notes: Graduated 1833 New York City. Medical degree.

career.notes: Held the chair of obstetrics until his death.

notable.notable_blurb: 19th-century Americana book collector; bequeathed Yale 12,000 volumes
notable.primary_url_label: William L. Clements Library — George Brinley Papers

narrative_blocks.header: Built one of America's greatest Americana libraries
narrative_blocks.body: He spent his life as a Hartford banker but began collecting Americana while still a student. Before his 1875 death he had amassed over 12,000 rare volumes — bequeathed in large part to Yale University, with the remainder auctioned 1878-1893.

narrative_blocks.header: Active in Episcopal Church and Trinity College
narrative_blocks.body: Throughout his life he was active in the Episcopal Church — in his own parish, supporting Trinity College, and in state and national conventions. He died in Hamilton, Bermuda on May 16, 1875, almost certainly while seeking a milder climate for failing health.

sources.label: William L. Clements Library — George Brinley Papers

education.notes: Began collecting Americana while a student.

institutions.institution_blurb: Active Episcopal layman who supported Trinity College throughout his life.

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Hartford CT (with parents and siblings)
West Laurel Hill Cemetery, Bala Cynwyd PA

narrative_blocks.header: 1918-19 flu: turned YWCA building into emergency hospital
narrative_blocks.body: During the 1918-1919 influenza pandemic, Ellen Terry Brinley's efforts led the Kiowa Street YWCA building in Colorado Springs — which she had personally led fundraising to build — to be turned over free of charge as an emergency hospital. Hundreds received care there during the worst weeks of the epidemic's local impact. The pivot from women's residence to public hospital during a public-health emergency was the most consequential single decision of her thirteen-year YWCA presidency.

narrative_blocks.header: Colorado Springs YWCA president thirteen years
narrative_blocks.body: Ellen Terry Brinley served as president of the Colorado Springs YWCA from 1908 through 1922 — thirteen years that included not just the wartime flu crisis but the long-planned Kiowa Street building fundraising. She resigned the presidency in December 1922 due to ill health and died of pneumonia in 1931. Prior to her presidency she had been a YWCA board director.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded Woman's Educational Society, St. Stephens parish
narrative_blocks.body: Ellen Brinley's civic portfolio also included co-founding the Woman's Educational Society of Colorado College — which funded a girls' dormitory — and founding St. Stephens Episcopal parish. She was also a director of Associated Charities, the Colorado Springs Public Library, the Coburn Library Book Club, and the El Paso County Humane Society. The complete board portfolio spanned the women's education, public welfare, library, and humane-society causes of early-twentieth-century Mountain West progressive philanthropy.

narrative_blocks.header: Hartford-born, Colorado Springs from early 1880s
narrative_blocks.body: Ellen Terry Brinley was born January 7, 1856 in Hartford Connecticut, youngest child of George Brinley Jr. and Frances Ellen Terry. She moved to Colorado Springs in the early 1880s with sister Catherine and brother-in-law Dr. Benjamin Faneuil Dunkin Adams II, living with their family until Catherine's death in 1905, then building her own home at 15 Beverly Place in 1910 where her niece Katherine Faneuil Adams lived with her. She died unmarried.

cross_connections.display_label: Edwards won his seat — then handed it back
Talcott cousins who both supplied Washington's army from CT
EH notes their Standley cousinship — before he married a Hooker
Raised him from age four after his father died
His grandmother Elizabeth Standley was sister to Abigail Standley Hooker.

notable.notable_blurb: Commissary General; Rochambeau's host; 2nd President Bank NY

narrative_blocks.header: Commissary General to both Washington and Rochambeau
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Commissary General of the Continental Army from April 1778, then assumed the same role for Rochambeau's entire French force when it arrived in July 1780. By 1781 he had marshaled 855 horses, 600 oxen, 239 wagon conductors, and bread ovens along the route from Newport to the Hudson — making the Yorktown campaign possible.

narrative_blocks.header: Washington and Rochambeau strategized in his Hartford parlor
narrative_blocks.body: On September 20-22, 1780, Washington and Rochambeau held their war strategy conference at his Hartford home, joined by Hamilton, Knox, and Lafayette. The conference ended abruptly when news of Benedict Arnold's treason at West Point reached them.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised by the uncle who became his brother-in-law
narrative_blocks.body: His uncle Matthew Talcott raised him from age four after his father died — and shipped him before the mast to improve his health. Years later he married Mehitable Russell, sister of Matthew Talcott's wife Mary, making his guardian also his brother-in-law.

narrative_blocks.header: Second president of the Bank of New York
narrative_blocks.body: He served as second President of the Bank of New York 1785-1786, working closely with his friend Alexander Hamilton on the early Republic's financial architecture. Edward Hooker called him 'one of the few men to whom the Revolutionary War proved a source of wealth.'

narrative_blocks.header: Father of the man who founded Wadsworth Atheneum
narrative_blocks.body: His son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford — the oldest continuously operating public art museum in America. The museum opened in 1844, four decades after Jeremiah's death.

narrative_blocks.header: Edwards beat him then handed back the seat
narrative_blocks.body: In 1790 Pierpont Edwards — fellow Hartford figure and Hooker descendant — defeated him for the Connecticut Congressional seat. Edwards then declined to take it, forcing a special election that returned Wadsworth to Congress.

narrative_blocks.header: WWII Liberty ship named in his honor
narrative_blocks.body: During World War II the United States Liberty ship SS Jeremiah Wadsworth was named in his honor. It was a fitting echo of his Revolutionary commissary work — Liberty ships served as workhorses of America's wartime supply effort, just as he had supplied Washington and Rochambeau.

institutions.institution_blurb: His 1784 portrait by John Trumbull (ART030) hangs in the Atheneum his son Daniel founded.
Part of Hartford's elite network that financed the Connecticut Land Company.

education.institution_blurb: Honorary degrees from Yale and Dartmouth, 1796.

cross_connections.display_label: He raised her future husband — and was already married to her sister

narrative_blocks.header: Her marriage united Hartford's two founding bloodlines
narrative_blocks.body: Mehitable Russell descended from Thomas Hooker through her mother Mary Pierpont Russell, granddaughter of Rev. James Pierpont and Mary Hooker. When she married Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth in 1767 — himself a Talcott descendant through his mother Abigail Talcott — their children became the convergence of the two men who had founded Hartford together in 1636: Thomas Hooker and John Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: The man who raised her husband had married
narrative_blocks.body: Mehitable's sister Mary Russell had married Matthew Talcott of Middletown in 1740. When Rev. Daniel Wadsworth died in 1747 — leaving four-year-old Jeremiah fatherless — Matthew took the boy in and raised him. So when Mehitable married Jeremiah in 1764, she married into a household she already knew: the home of her own brother-in-law.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived her husband by thirteen years
narrative_blocks.body: Jeremiah Wadsworth died on April 30, 1804, at age 60. Mehitable lived until March 29, 1817 — another thirteen years, to age 82. She outlived her daughter Harriet, who had died in Bermuda at 23.

narrative_blocks.header: Cousins twice over before the wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband descended from Elizabeth Standley, whose sister Abigail had married John Hooker of Farmington. The Standley sisters meant the Wadsworth and Hooker lines were already kin — her marriage doubled a connection a century old.

institutions.institution_blurb: Mother of Daniel Wadsworth (HD1330), who founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.

bio.bio_blurb: Wife of the Revolution’s Commissary General

cross_connections.display_label: His mother's sister married into the Hooker family

narrative_blocks.header: His uncle hid Connecticut's Charter in the Oak
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Daniel Wadsworth's uncle Joseph Wadsworth was the man who seized the Connecticut Charter on the night of October 31, 1687 and hid it in the Charter Oak in Hartford — aided by Lt.-Col. John Talcott, the grandfather of Daniel's future wife Abigail Talcott. Daniel himself had no part in it; he was born 17 years later. But both families whose founding myth was the Charter Oak — Wadsworth and Talcott — converged in his household.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at 43
narrative_blocks.body: Daniel Wadsworth died on November 12, 1747, when his son Jeremiah was four years old. The boy was sent to be raised by his Talcott uncle Matthew Talcott of Middletown — his mother Abigail's brother. The man who would become Commissary General of the Continental Army and intimate friend of Washington grew up in a Talcott household, knowing his father's family only by reputation.

education.institution_blurb: Yale College 1726. Member of Yale Corporation 1743 until his death in 1747.

narrative_blocks.header: Her four daughters never married; only the son
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Talcott, daughter of Connecticut governor Joseph Talcott, married the Reverend Daniel Wadsworth, Hartford's pastor and a member of the Yale Corporation. Of their six children, the four daughters who survived infancy all died unmarried; only her son Jeremiah, who wed Mehitable Russell, carried the family forward.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Hamlin Russell — her sister Mehitable Hamlin married Samuel Hooker Jr., making Rev. William Russell's aunt the wife of Mary Pierpont's maternal uncle. EH notes this double family connection explicitly.
parents.father_research_notes: Rev. Noadiah Russell of Middletown — founding trustee of Yale College

church_roles.notes: Successor to his father Rev. Noadiah Russell

cross_connections.display_label: Married the sister of his own sister's husband, both Pierpont-Russell siblings.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of Yale's founding trustee Noadiah Russell
narrative_blocks.body: His father Rev. Noadiah Russell was one of the ten founding trustees of Yale College in 1701 — the same founding cohort as Rev. James Pierpont Sr (his future father-in-law). Two Yale founding-trustee families thus merged through his marriage to Mary Pierpont in 1718.

narrative_blocks.header: Double Hamlin family connection to Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: His mother Mary Hamlin's sister Mehitable Hamlin had married Samuel Hooker Jr. - brother of Mary Pierpont's mother Mary Hooker. Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy notes this double family connection explicitly: two Hamlin sisters married into the Hooker-Pierpont line one generation apart.

narrative_blocks.header: Pastor of Middletown First Church for decades
narrative_blocks.body: Served as pastor of Middletown First Church through the mid-18th century until his death 1 June 1761 at age 70. Father of nine surviving children — five sons + four daughters — through whom the Russell name carried into the Wadsworth, Talcott, Anthony, and Dwight branches of the Hooker tree.

narrative_blocks.header: Forty-six years in the pulpit, to the day
narrative_blocks.body: Settled as Middletown’s pastor on June 1, 1715, William Russell died on June 1, 1761 — forty-six years in the same pulpit to the day.

cross_connections.display_label: Both families joined through Talcott siblings a generation later.
Her daughter's marriage to Talcott converged founding lines.

institutions.institution_blurb: Father Rev. James Pierpont Sr. and father-in-law Rev. Noadiah Russell were both founding trustees of Yale in the same 1701 charter.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father and father-in-law both founded Yale
narrative_blocks.body: She was the daughter of Rev. James Pierpont Sr., one of the ten founding trustees who chartered Yale College on October 16, 1701. She married Rev. William Russell, son of Rev. Noadiah Russell of Middletown — who was also one of those ten founding trustees.

narrative_blocks.header: Four founding families converge in her children's generation
narrative_blocks.body: Through her parents she carried the Hooker line and the Pierpont-Yale line. Through marriage she joined the Russell-Yale line. Her daughter Mary Russell married Matthew Talcott — adding the Talcott founding line to the convergence her children embodied.

narrative_blocks.header: A hidden Hooker-Hamlin connection in her husband's family
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. William Russell's mother Mary Hamlin was the sister of Mehitable Hamlin, who married Samuel Hooker Jr. — a brother of Mary Pierpont's own mother. This made William Russell's aunt the wife of Mary's uncle, drawing a second bond between the families before their own marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Hartford's founder — nine children
narrative_blocks.body: Her grandfather was Rev. Thomas Hooker, who led the 1636 migration from Cambridge that founded Hartford. She settled in Middletown with her husband and bore nine children: Mary, Esther, William, Samuel, Noadiah, James, Sarah, Mehitable, and Hannah.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter Mary married Matthew Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Mary Russell married Matthew Talcott of Hartford — the marriage that created the Russell-Talcott convergence. Their children were both Hooker and Talcott descendants, connecting two of colonial Connecticut's founding families.

narrative_blocks.header: She may have stitched the family apron
narrative_blocks.body: Of the three Marys linked to a surviving 1730s silk apron, she is one of two likeliest embroiderers, depending on which attended a Boston finishing school and when. The apron’s Chinese silk and French metallic thread mark the family’s reach into global trade.

bio.bio_blurb: Mother of the Russell nine at Middletown
